source_paper_title: Near-Optimal Bayesian Online Assortment of Reusable Resources
context:
Section 4 (Conclusion), page 21

On the technical side, the most immediate problem stemming from our work is finding the optimal competitive ratio for the case of general rental duration distributions. In particular, can one shave the logarithmic factor in our competitive ratio and obtain a \(1 - O(1/\sqrt{c_{\min}})\) competitive algorithm, similar to the best known competitive ratio in the non-reusable case?
title: Remove logarithmic factor in competitive ratio for reusable-resource Bayesian online assortment
background_latex:
Consider a finite-horizon Bayesian online assortment problem with reusable resources.

1) Products and inventories. There are \(n\) rental products indexed by \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Product \(i\) has initial inventory \(c_i\in\mathbb Z_+\). Let \(c_{\min}=\min_{i\in[n]} c_i\).

2) Time and types. Consumers arrive at times \(t=1,\dots,T\). At time \(t\), a type \(z_t\) is drawn independently from a known distribution \(F_t\) over a finite type space \(\mathcal Z_t\), and the realized type is observed upon arrival.

3) Choice models and feasible assortments. For each type \(z\), the consumer choice behavior is described by a function \(\phi^{z}(S,i)\in[0,1]\), interpreted as the probability that a type-\(z\) consumer chooses product \(i\in[n]\) when offered assortment \(S\). Feasible assortments (ignoring availability) form a downward-closed family \(\mathcal S\subseteq 2^{[n]}\): if \(S\in\mathcal S\) and \(S'\subseteq S\), then \(S'\in\mathcal S\). Choice models satisfy weak substitutability: for all types \(z\), all \(S\in\mathcal S\), and all distinct \(i,j\in[n]\), \(\phi^{z}(S,i)\ge \phi^{z}(S\cup\{j\},i)\), and \(\phi^{z}(\varnothing,i)=0\).

4) Rental fees and rental durations. If a type-\(z\) consumer chooses product \(i\), the platform earns a one-time rental fee \(r_i^{z}\ge 0\). The rented unit is occupied for a random integer duration \(d\in\mathbb Z_+\) drawn independently from a known distribution \(G_i^{z}\) (with pmf \(g_i^{z}\)). The unit returns to inventory after \(d\) periods.

5) Online policy and feasibility. An online policy observes the history and the current type \(z_t\), offers an available assortment \(S_t\in\mathcal S\) consisting only of products with at least one available unit, and then a random choice is realized according to \(\phi^{z_t}(S_t,\cdot)\). The objective is expected total revenue \(\mathbb E[\sum_{t=1}^T r^{z_t}_{i_t}]\).

6) Bayesian expected-LP benchmark. For a given sequence \(\{F_t\}_{t=1}^T\), define decision variables \(y_{S,t,z}\ge 0\) intended as the probability of offering assortment \(S\in\mathcal S\) at time \(t\) when the realized type is \(z\in\mathcal Z_t\). The Bayesian expected linear-program benchmark \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}[\{F_t\}_{t=1}^T]\) is the optimal value of
\[
\max_{y\ge 0}\ \sum_{t=1}^T \sum_{z\in\mathcal Z_t} \sum_{S\in\mathcal S} F_t(z)\sum_{i=1}^n r_i^{z}\,\phi^{z}(S,i)\, y_{S,t,z}
\]
subject to, for every product \(i\in[n]\) and time \(t\in[T]\), the expected number of units of product \(i\) in use at time \(t\) is at most \(c_i\):
\[
\sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \sum_{z\in\mathcal Z_{\tau}} \sum_{S\in\mathcal S} F_{\tau}(z)\,\bar G_i^{z}(t-\tau)\,\phi^{z}(S,i)\, y_{S,\tau,z} \le c_i,
\]
where \(\bar G_i^{z}(u)=1-G_i^{z}(u)\), and also \(\sum_{S\in\mathcal S} y_{S,t,z}\le 1\) for all \(t\) and \(z\in\mathcal Z_t\).

7) Competitive ratio against \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}\). For an online algorithm \(\mathcal A\), let \(\mathrm{Rev}_{\mathcal A}[\{F_t\}]\) denote its expected total revenue under \(\{F_t\}_{t=1}^T\). The competitive ratio (against \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}\)) is
\[
\inf_{T\ge 1}\ \inf_{\{F_t\}_{t=1}^T}\ \frac{\mathrm{Rev}_{\mathcal A}[\{F_t\}]}{\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}[\{F_t\}] }.
\]

Known bound motivating the question: for general rental-duration distributions, there is a polynomial-time algorithm with competitive ratio \(1-\min\{1/2,\,O(\sqrt{\log(c_{\min})/c_{\min}})\}\) against \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}\); for the non-reusable (infinite-duration) case, the best known competitive ratio scales like \(1-O(1/\sqrt{c_{\min}})\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether, for the Bayesian online assortment problem with reusable resources and general rental-duration distributions, the logarithmic factor in the known large-inventory competitive ratio can be removed.

Concretely: is there a polynomial-time online algorithm whose competitive ratio against \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}[\{F_t\}]\) scales as
\[
1 - O\!
\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{c_{\min}}}\right)
\]
in the large-inventory regime (i.e., as \(c_{\min}\to\infty\)), improving over the \(1-O(\sqrt{\log(c_{\min})/c_{\min}})\) rate currently obtained for general rental-duration distributions?

Determine whether, for the Bayesian online assortment problem with reusable resources and general rental-duration distributions, the logarithmic factor in the known large-inventory competitive ratio can be removed.

Concretely: is there a polynomial-time online algorithm whose competitive ratio against \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}[\{F_t\}]\) scales as
\[
1 - O\!
\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{c_{\min}}}\right)
\]
in the large-inventory regime (i.e., as \(c_{\min}\to\infty\)), improving over the \(1-O(\sqrt{\log(c_{\min})/c_{\min}})\) rate currently obtained for general rental-duration distributions?
open_problem_quote: On the technical side, the most immediate problem stemming from our work is finding the optimal competitive ratio for the case of general rental duration distributions. In particular, can one shave the logarithmic factor in our competitive ratio and obtain a \(1 - O(1/\sqrt{c_{\min}})\) competitive algorithm, similar to the best known competitive ratio in the non-reusable case?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is about existence of a better algorithmic guarantee rather than falsifying a universal inequality. However, automated search over small instances could help test candidate policies and potentially suggest lower-bound instances indicating that a logarithmic term might be necessary for certain algorithm classes, though translating this into a proof is nontrivial.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate online policies (e.g., parameterized discarding/thresholding rules combined with LP-guided sampling) can be represented in code and evaluated via Monte Carlo on sampled instances to estimate worst-case ratios against the computable \(\mathrm{Expected\text{-}LP}\) value. The fitness signal is noisy and instance-dependent, but could guide discovery of improved heuristics or suggest conjectured constants/rates.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The paper of Feng–Niazadeh–Saberi formulates Bayesian online assortment with reusable inventories and type-dependent choice and rental durations, and benchmarks performance against a Bayesian Expected-LP that enforces inventory feasibility only in expectation. Their near-optimal algorithmic framework (solve Expected-LP, simulate, then enforce sample-path feasibility via per-product discarding plus a sub-assortment sampling post-processing step) achieves competitive ratio \(1-\min\{1/2,\,O(\sqrt{\log(c_{\min})/c_{\min}})\}\) for general rental-duration distributions. The logarithmic factor arises from a concentration/slack argument needed to control inventory violations under reuse and correlations across time, even after the sub-assortment sampling step restores the LP’s marginal choice probabilities under weak substitutability.

Among the (currently provided) forward citations, “Stationary Online Contention Resolution Schemes” develops a stationary OCRS framework with an LP characterization and universal online implementation, and it reports \(1-\Theta(1/\sqrt{k})\) stationary selectability for recurring \(k\)-uniform matroid-type environments, improving over earlier \(1-\Theta(\sqrt{\log k/k})\) losses in related reusable-resource abstractions. While it does not directly address assortment with weak-substitute choice and stochastic rental durations, it supplies a potentially relevant rounding/selection primitive: if the reusable-resource assortment problem (or its discarding/feasibility subproblem) can be cast as a recurring matroid/packing selection problem with a stationary implementable distribution, then stationary OCRS techniques might remove the \(\sqrt{\log c/c}\) term and yield \(1-O(1/\sqrt{c_{\min}})\) behavior.

Given the limited forward-citation evidence supplied here, the specific open problem—removing the logarithmic factor for general rental-duration distributions in Bayesian online assortment against Expected-LP—appears to remain open. The most promising direction suggested by the citation is to replace the paper’s independent randomized discarding plus Chernoff-style slack with a stationary, globally coupled selection/rounding scheme (in the spirit of stationary OCRSs) that achieves optimal \(1-\Theta(1/\sqrt{c})\) selectability for recurring capacity constraints, while still preserving the marginal choice probabilities ensured by sub-assortment sampling under weak substitutability.

sources.title: Stationary Online Contention Resolution Schemes
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces stationary online contention resolution schemes (S-OCRSs), characterized exactly by a “stationary OCRS LP” over distributions \(\mu\in\Delta(\mathcal F)\) on feasible sets with constraints: (i) selectability \(\Pr_{S\sim\mu}[e\in S]\ge \alpha x_e\) for all \(e\), and (ii) stationary implementability \(\Pr_{S\sim\mu}[e\in S\mid S\setminus\{e\}=T]\le x_e\) for all \(e\) and feasible \(T\not\ni e\). Any feasible \(\mu\) can be implemented online for any arrival order via a universal “simulate-then-replace” algorithm that preserves \(\mathrm{Law}(S)=\mu\). Using a maximum-entropy/Gibbs construction, they obtain explicit optimal S-OCRSs for several environments, notably for the \(k\)-uniform matroid with selectability \(\alpha_k=\Pr[Q<k\mid Q\le k]=1-\sqrt{2/(\pi k)}+O(1/k)\) where \(Q\sim\mathrm{Poisson}(k)\), and for bipartite matchings with selectability \(\alpha^*=(3-\sqrt5)/2\), optimal within the stationary class.
sources.connection: The open problem asks to remove a \(\sqrt{\log c_{\min}/c_{\min}}\) term in the competitive ratio for Bayesian online assortment with reusable resources and general rental durations, aiming for \(1-O(1/\sqrt{c_{\min}})\) against an Expected-LP benchmark. This paper does not analyze that assortment model or prove such a competitive ratio. However, it develops S-OCRSs motivated by reusable/recurring resources and states that in a companion paper (Aminian et al., 2026) the stationarity property yields improved guarantees for Bayesian online allocation with reusable resources, improving over a prior \(1-\Theta(\sqrt{\log k/k})\) bound to \(\alpha_k=1-\Theta(1/\sqrt{k})\) for a recurring \(k\)-uniform matroid abstraction. Thus, this paper provides a key technical framework (stationary OCRS LP + Gibbs/maximum-entropy constructions) that could be used as an admission-control/rounding primitive in reusable-resource revenue management, potentially relevant to removing logarithmic factors, but it does not itself establish the desired \(1-O(1/\sqrt{c_{\min}})\) competitive ratio for the stated assortment problem with general rental durations.
